Tutorial 2: Building Toy Worlds (Interactive Notebook)

What a simulation is, and why scientists trust dice

  • How to use this notebook
  • Word list（単語リスト）
  • What is a model?
  • Experiment 1: How fair is a coin?
  • Try it! — how surprising is 9 heads?
  • Experiment 2: More flips, more truth
  • Writing our own function
  • Experiment 3: The random walk
  • Try it! — biased walkers
  • What you learned

How to use this notebook

Open ScienceHighSchool.Rproj first. Run each code block with the green Run button, or place your cursor in the code and press Cmd/Ctrl + Enter.

Change one small thing at a time. Then run the block again and look at the result.

Word list（単語リスト）

English 日本語 Meaning
simulation シミュレーション letting a model “run” inside a computer
model モデル a simplified copy of something real
probability 確率 how likely something is (0 = never, 1 = always)
loop ループ・繰り返し telling R to repeat something many times
random walk ランダムウォーク a path built from random steps

What is a model?

A map of Tokyo is not Tokyo. It leaves out almost everything — and that’s exactly why it is useful.

A model is the same idea in science: a simplified copy of the real world that keeps only the parts we care about. A simulation is what happens when we let a model run inside a computer, like pressing “play”.

This week our models will be about people (and animals) learning and copying each other. But first, let’s build the simplest possible toy world.

Experiment 1: How fair is a coin?

If you flip a fair coin 10 times, do you get exactly 5 heads?

flips <- sample(c(1, 0), size = 10, replace = TRUE)  # 1 = heads, 0 = tails
flips
 [1] 0 0 0 1 1 1 0 1 0 1
sum(flips)   # how many heads?
[1] 5
flips <- sample(c(1, 0), size = 10, replace = TRUE)  # 1 = heads, 0 = tails
flips
 [1] 1 1 1 1 1 0 0 1 1 0
sum(flips)   # how many heads?
[1] 7

Run this a few times. Sometimes 3 heads, sometimes 7… Now the magic of simulation: we don’t have to flip by hand.

n_heads <- replicate(10000, sum(sample(c(1, 0), size = 10, replace = TRUE)))

replicate(10000, ...) repeats our little experiment ten thousand times and collects the results. Let’s see them all:

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data.frame(n_heads), aes(x = n_heads)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "tomato") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:10) +
  labs(title = "10,000 repetitions of '10 coin flips'",
       x = "number of heads", y = "count")

Try it! — how surprising is 9 heads?

Using the vector n_heads:

mean(n_heads >= 9)

This computes the fraction of experiments with 9 or 10 heads. If a friend flips 10 coins and gets 9 heads, should you suspect a trick coin?

(This — estimating a probability by simulating many times — is called the Monte Carlo method. Real researchers use it constantly.)

Experiment 2: More flips, more truth

flip_average <- function(n_flips) {
  mean(sample(c(1, 0), size = n_flips, replace = TRUE))
}

results <- data.frame(
  n = c(10, 100, 1000, 10000),
  proportion_heads = c(flip_average(10), flip_average(100),
                       flip_average(1000), flip_average(10000))
)
results

Small samples are wild; big samples settle near 0.5. This is the law of large numbers — and it is one reason why averaging many opinions can be powerful. Remember this for Day 3: it is the secret behind the “wisdom of crowds”.

Writing our own function

We just did something important: we wrote our own function, flip_average(). A function is a recipe:

name <- function(ingredients) {
  ... what to do ...
}

From now on we will build all our models as functions.

Experiment 3: The random walk

Imagine a person standing on a line. Every second they flip a coin: heads → one step left (+1), tails → one step right (-1). Where are they after 100 steps?

steps <- sample(c(1, -1), size = 100, replace = TRUE)
position <- c(0, cumsum(steps))   # cumulative sum: position after each step
ggplot(data.frame(time = 0:100, position), aes(time, position)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "A random walk")

Run it again — a completely different path every time! Now let’s watch 20 walkers at once:

random_walk <- function(n_steps = 100) {
  c(0, cumsum(sample(c(1, -1), size = n_steps, replace = TRUE)))
}

walks <- data.frame(
  time = rep(0:100, times = 20),
  position = unlist(lapply(1:20, function(i) random_walk(100))),
  walker = rep(1:20, each = 101)
)

ggplot(walks, aes(time, position, group = walker)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.4, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "20 random walkers")

Each walker is different, but the crowd of walkers has a clear shape: it spreads out like a fan. Individual randomness, collective pattern. That is the core idea of everything we do this week.

Try it! — biased walkers

  1. Change the walk so that steps are +1 with probability 0.6 and -1 with probability 0.4 (hint: prob = c(0.6, 0.4) inside sample()).
  2. Plot 20 of these walkers. What changed about the fan?
  3. A stock price, a drunk person, an animal searching for food — which of these could a random walk be a model of? What does the model leave out in each case?

What you learned

  • A model is a deliberately simplified toy world; a simulation runs it
  • replicate() and functions let us repeat experiments thousands of times
  • Small samples lie; large samples tell the truth (law of large numbers)
  • Simple random rules can create clear collective patterns

Next tutorial: our toy world gets its first inhabitant — an agent that can learn.
